title: OEE Framework
body: At ADW, the trucks and excavators post 90%+ mechanical availability — yet output consistently falls short. The gap lives in Performance and Quality losses that the current workbooks capture but nobody has combined into a single number you can act on.
keyPoints: OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality. World-class manufacturing hits 85%; load-and-haul mining targets 65–70% as a realistic benchmark.

Availability tracks whether equipment is mechanically ready. Performance tracks whether it's working at full cycle tempo when it is ready. Quality tracks whether the output actually went to the right place.

The Performance loss at ADW — dump trucks running 15–25 points below availability — comes from "no location," "waiting for excavator," and standby reasons, not breakdowns. That's a dispatch and face-management problem, not a maintenance problem.
Wrong-tipping events and crusher bin-level stoppages are Quality failures in OEE terms. A truck that completed a cycle but dumped at the wrong pad produced zero good output for that cycle.
The data to compute OEE already exists: A&U workbook (Availability), running-hours log (Performance), daily production report (Quality proxy). It just hasn't been combined.
apply: If you're tracking truck delays at the face during shift, note the standby reason code — "waiting for excavator" vs "no dig location" — because those two categories need different fixes, and separating them is the first step to closing the Performance gap.
